proposedPrinciple: Concurrency must respect review throughput. Do not scale agent fan-out beyond the human, test, and merge capacity that can inspect the results. An agent system should use queues, backpressure, smaller batches, or stricter acceptance gates before parallel activity outruns accountable review.
durabilityReason: The lesson is model- and vendor-neutral: any multi-agent system can create more concurrent work than its accountable review path can safely absorb.
additiveRationale: Existing Agent Principles says parallelism needs legibility; this candidate adds an explicit capacity rule tying fan-out to human, test, and merge review throughput.

proposedPrinciple: Externalize load-bearing intent, not only state. Goals, constraints, rationale, non-negotiables, and decision records should live in artifacts that agents can read. If the reason a system behaves a certain way exists only in a human head, cold-start agents will guess at it, multiply drift, and make fluent changes against the wrong target.
durabilityReason: The lesson is vendor-neutral and durable: any cold-start agent system becomes less reliable when goals, constraints, and rationale exist only as tacit human memory instead of inspectable artifacts.
additiveRationale: Existing Agent Principles already externalizes state and judges against intent; this adds a distinct rule that intent itself is a first-class artifact class for future agents.

proposedPrinciple: Design loops as control planes, not prompt chains. Recurring agent systems should discover work, route it, verify it, persist state, and decide the next action through explicit loop machinery instead of relying on repeated human prompts.
durabilityReason: The lesson is durable and vendor-neutral, but current Agent Principles already cover engineering the agent system rather than only the prompt, treating the loop as a kernel, using durable state, separating roles, making long runs restartable, and respecting review throughput.
additiveRationale: Rejected because the proposed wording would restate existing operating-posture, memory, long-running-action, and evaluation principles rather than add a distinct rule.
